title: Asymptotically Optimal Depth Fermionic Permutation on 2D Grid Quantum Architecture without Ancillas
authors: Dantong Li, Shifan Xu, Yongshan Ding
abstract: Simulating fermionic systems on qubit hardware involves many nonlocal interactions, and efficient routing of these interactions is critical to the overall cost of fermionic simulation algorithms. Recent works reduce this Jordan-Wigner routing overhead to polylogarithmic depth under all-to-all connectivity, but degrade to $O(\sqrt{N}\,\mathrm{polylog}\,N)$ for $N$ fermionic modes on 2D nearest-neighbor architectures. We present a fermionic permutation protocol tailored to 2D grid architectures that achieves the optimal $O(\sqrt{N})$ depth with $O(N\sqrt{N})$ nearest-neighbor gates and no ancilla qubits, mid-circuit measurements, or classical feedforward. This matches the $Ω(\sqrt{N})$ lower bound, which holds even when $O(N)$ ancillas and classical feedforward are permitted. We further construct an $O(\sqrt{N})$-depth transformation between the Jordan-Wigner, Bravyi-Kitaev, and Parity encodings on the 2D grid via a Hilbert-curve layout, extending our result to all three encodings. Benchmarks on the fermionic fast Fourier transform and Trotter simulation of sparse SYK model demonstrate consistent reduction in depth, spacetime volume, and infidelity for system sizes $N \gtrsim 100$ in the early fault-tolerant regime.
summary: This paper presents an optimal method for performing fermionic permutations on 2D quantum grids. By using a specialized routing and phase-correction strategy, it achieves the theoretical minimum circuit depth without needing extra ancilla qubits or complex classical feedback. This significantly reduces the resource requirements for simulating fermionic systems in the early fault-tolerant era.
main_problem: Efficiently implementing fermionic permutations on 2D nearest-neighbor quantum architectures without incurring the high depth overhead caused by non-local parity strings.
main_result: A protocol that achieves asymptotically optimal O(sqrt(N)) depth and O(N*sqrt(N)) gate count without using ancilla qubits, mid-circuit measurements, or classical feedforward.
method: The authors use a three-stage Row-Column-Row (RCR) decomposition with an Odd-Even Transposition sort and a diagonal Gamma operator to resolve non-local phase corrections.
model_or_system: A 2D grid quantum architecture with nearest-neighbor connectivity, utilizing Jordan-Wigner, Bravyi-Kitaev, and Parity fermion-to-qubit encodings.
key_observables: CNOT depth, gate count, spacetime volume, and circuit infidelity.
important_parameters: The early fault-tolerant regime, specifically for system sizes N >= 100.
main_assumptions: The architecture is a fixed 2D grid with nearest-neighbor connectivity and a 'snake' Jordan-Wigner ordering.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the snake-like ordering on a grid; Figure 5 illustrates the four-phase structure of the Gamma operator circuit; Figure 6 shows the ternary tree encoding; Figure 7 shows the transformation pipeline between encodings; Figure 8 shows the Hilbert-curve-based routing.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the routing problem, presents the RCR decomposition protocol, details the ancilla-free Gamma operator construction, extends the results to BK and Parity encodings via Hilbert curves, and concludes with numerical benchmarks on FFFT and the SYK model.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Snake JW order on an L × L grid. Vertical swaps cross the row turn, making them nonlocal in JW order and need phase corrections along the chain.
FIG. 2: Odd–even transposition example on one row: alternating rounds swap adjacent pairs until the target permutation is achieved.
FIG. 3: Overview of the ancilla-free fermionic permu- tation protocol. The five stages execute sequentially; the Γ sandwich converts bare vertical FSWAPs into full fermionic SWAPs.
FIG. 4: Hall’s Row-Column-Row decomposition: RowA routes within source rows, the column stage moves within columns to destination rows, and RowB routes within destination rows to final columns.

FIG. 5: The ancilla-free Γ circuit. (a, b) Fused sweeps: a single prefix CNOT cascade on an even row absorbs mul- tiple T(x, y) interactions as trailing operations at fixed column offsets, achieving depth 2L+O(1) per sweep. In fused sweep A, the skip-row 4-gate gadget routes interactions through an intermediary row; in fused sweep B, cross- row interactions use direct vertical CZ gates. (c) The four-phase structure of Algorithm 2. Phase 2 requires two batches because skip-row gadgets from group r access row r+2, which is the cascade row of group r+2. Phase 4 needs no batching since cross-row CZ gates do not modify the target row.
